name: Cherokee Purple
image_alt: A deep dusky-purple beefsteak tomato with green shoulders
description: One of the most beloved heirloom tomatoes, Cherokee Purple produces large, flattened fruits with a distinctive dusky rose-purple color and dark green shoulders. The flavor is exceptionally rich and complex — sweet with a smoky, wine-like depth that sets it apart from most tomatoes.
history: Believed to have been grown by the Cherokee people for centuries, this variety was shared with gardener Craig LeHoullier in 1990 by a Tennessee family who had maintained it for generations. It quickly became a staple of the heirloom tomato revival.

good.reason: Repels aphids and whiteflies, may improve flavor

bad.reason: Inhibits growth of most vegetables including tomatoes
Compete for nutrients and may stunt tomato growth

seasonal_quirks: Sets fruit poorly when temperatures exceed 95°F. Flavor peaks after the first cool nights in late summer. The purple coloring deepens with warmth and sun exposure.
pests_diseases: blossom end rot if watering is inconsistent
harvest_cues: Shoulders remain green even when ripe — check the bottom for dusky rose color and a slight give. Don't wait for the shoulders to turn.
variety_notes: Among the 'black' tomato types alongside Paul Robeson and Black Krim. More complex and smoky than most reds. Fruits average 10–12 oz but can exceed a pound.
preservation: Best fresh — complex flavor diminishes somewhat when cooked. Can be frozen whole or roasted and frozen.
